perturbation.original_text: 21.1 Distributor shall obtain all licenses, permits and approvals required by any government, including any recycling or take-back programs applicable to packaging or Products, and shall comply with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, policies and procedures and any requirements applicable to the importation, exportation, use, sale, loan, purchase, and distribution of Products under telecommunications, consumer rights related, environmental, labor, tax, and any other laws and regulations, of any government or other competent authority where the Products are to be sold, used or deployed (collectively "Applicable Laws").
perturbation.changed_text: 21.1 Distributor shall obtain all licenses, permits and approvals required by any government, including any recycling or take-back programs applicable to packaging or Products, and shall comply with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, policies and procedures and any requirements applicable to the importation, exportation, use, sale, loan, purchase, and distribution of Products under telecommunications, environmental, labor, tax, and any other laws and regulations, of any government or other competent authority where the Products are to be sold, used or deployed (collectively "Applicable Laws").
perturbation.explanation: By removing "consumer rights related" from the list of laws that the distributor must comply with, the contract becomes non-compliant with consumer protection laws. This omission creates a legal loophole, allowing the distributor to potentially disregard legally mandated consumer rights within the applicable territory (US, excluding its territories).
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act ensures that consumers receive clear and complete information regarding product warranties. By omitting the explicit requirement to comply with 'consumer rights related' laws, the distributor may fail to adequately provide warranty information or honor consumer warranty rights, directly contradicting the Act's purpose.

perturbation.justification: The omission of "consumer rights related" laws directly undermines consumer protection mandates, creating a legal loophole as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation also argues that the omission might lead to failure in providing warranty information or honoring consumer warranty rights, potentially contradicting the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. Even with the absence of a usable scraped law snippet, the core issue is clear: removing the explicit requirement to comply with consumer rights creates a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 C i s c o P r o d u c t s a r e p r o v i d e d w i t h w r i t t e n l i m i t e d w a r r a n t i e s , a s s e t f o r t h a t t h e f o l l o w i n g U R L : http://www.cisco.com/warp/public/cc/serv/mkt/sup/tsssv/wnty/. Distributor will pass through to Resellers all written limited warranties provided by Cisco with Products purchased by Distributor and Distributor shall contractually require (in whatever manner Distributor deems appropriate) each such Reseller to provide such warranty and other terms to any End User to which the Reseller redistributes the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 C i s c o P r o d u c t s a r e p r o v i d e d w i t h w r i t t e n l i m i t e d w a r r a n t i e s . Distributor will pass through to Resellers all written limited warranties provided by Cisco with Products purchased by Distributor and Distributor shall contractually require (in whatever manner Distributor deems appropriate) each such Reseller to provide such warranty and other terms to any End User to which the Reseller redistributes the Product.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the URL that points to Cisco's written limited warranties, it makes it difficult for the Distributor and subsequent Resellers/End Users to actually know what the warranties entail. This violates consumer protection laws that mandate clear warranty information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires that warranties be designated as either 'full' or 'limited' and that the warranty terms are made available to the consumer prior to the sale. Removing the specific URL makes it difficult for the distributor/reseller to properly convey this information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the URL pointing to Cisco's warranties creates a legal contradiction by removing essential information, specifically warranty details. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this makes it difficult for distributors, resellers, and end users to understand the warranty terms, potentially violating consumer protection laws mandating clear warranty information. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires warranty terms to be available before the sale. The changed text's omission directly undermines this requirement. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Document Not Found' error, Scraped Law Snippet 1 is valid, and the other information is sufficient to determine that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 25.0 INSURANCE (a) Each party shall be responsible for maintaining Worker's Compensation insurance in the statutory amounts required by the applicable state laws. (b) Each party shall maintain Commercial General Liability insurance with bodily injury and property damage limits of $[*****] per occurrence and $[*****] aggregate. Such insurance shall (a) provide for contractual liability coverage, (b) provide for cross liability coverage, and (c) name the other party and its subcontractors, as well as the directors, officers, employees, agents, successors and assigns of all of them, as additional insureds, but only to the extent of liabilities falling within the indemnity obligations of the other party pursuant to the terms of Section 24 in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 25.0 INSURANCE (a) Each party shall be responsible for maintaining Worker's Compensation insurance. (b) Each party shall maintain Commercial General Liability insurance. Such insurance shall (a) provide for contractual liability coverage, (b) provide for cross liability coverage, and (c) name the other party and its subcontractors, as well as the directors, officers, employees, agents, successors and assigns of all of them, as additional insureds, but only to the extent of liabilities falling within the indemnity obligations of the other party pursuant to the terms of Section 24 in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clauses that provide requirements for statutory amounts for Worker's Compensation, and the specific insurance limits, the Distributor/Cisco will potentially fail to maintain the legally required minimum insurance coverage, which violates state laws and creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 3700 mandates that all employers secure the payment of workers' compensation. Removing the requirement for statutory amounts jeopardizes the employer's compliance, directly violating California Labor Code.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION AND INSURANCE [3200 - 6002] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1979, Ch. 373. ) PART 1. SCOPE AND OPERATION [3200 - 4418] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 4. Compensation Insurance and Security [3700 - 3823] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. Insurance and Security [3700 - 3709.5] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 3700. Every employer except the state shall secure the payment of compensation in one or more of the following ways: (a) By being insured against liability to pay compensation by one or more insurers duly authorized to write compensation insurance in this state. (b) By securing from the Director of Industrial Relations a certificate of consent to self-insure either as an individual employer, or as one employer in a group of employers, which may be given upon furnishing proof satisfactory to the Director of Industrial Relations of ability to self-insure and to pay any compensation that may become due to his or her employees. (c) For any county, city, city and county, municipal corporation, public district, public agency, or any political subdivision of the state, including each member of a pooling arrangement under a joint exercise of powers agreement (but not
perturbation.justification: The original text includes specific requirements for Worker's Compensation insurance (statutory amounts) and Commercial General Liability insurance (specific limits). The changed text omits these specific requirements, potentially leading to non-compliance with state laws regarding minimum insurance coverage, as the employer is not explicitly bound to those minimums in the changed text. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Labor Code Section 3700, mandates that employers secure workers' compensation payments. The Perturbation Explanation states that by removing the statutory amount requirements, the employer may fail to maintain legally required minimum insurance coverage. The omission in the changed text directly contradicts the legal obligation to secure worker's compensation by removing the explicit requirement to adhere to 'statutory amounts,' thus creating a legal contradiction.
